Gerarda Westerhuis. Sector economist Retail and Leisure
Guidelines and legislation on social impact. Companies have a responsibility to respect human rights according to the 2011 United Nations Guiding Principles on Business and Human Rights (UNGPs). This means that they must identify, prevent, reduce and counter the negative social impact that their activities directly or indirectly cause on human rights. The principles are based on the Universal Declaration of Human Rights (1948) and, together with the OECD guidelines, form the standard for International Corporate Social Responsibility (ICSR).

Guidelines and legislation on social impact. Companies have a responsibility to respect human rights according to the 2011 United Nations Guiding Principles on Business and Human Rights (UNGPs). This means that they must identify, prevent, reduce and counter the negative social impact that their activities directly or indirectly cause on human rights. The principles are based on the Universal Declaration of Human Rights (1948) and, together with the OECD guidelines, form the standard for International Corporate Social Responsibility (ICSR). By following the OECD guidelines, a company contributes to the seventeen Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) of the United Nations.

The principles are based on the Universal Declaration of Human Rights (1948) and, together with the OECD guidelines, form the standard for International Corporate Social Responsibility (ICSR). By following the OECD guidelines, a company contributes to the seventeen Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) of the United Nations. These goals form global ambitions for a sustainable world in 2030.
